points.text: Assign each webhook a unique event ID and use it as an idempotency key with a DB unique constraint.
Wrap charge and state update in a single DB transaction that first checks the idempotency key.
Only return HTTP 200 after the transaction commits; on failure return 5xx so the sender retries.
Set a short handler timeout (e.g., 5 seconds) and use exponential backoff for retrying failed webhooks.
Show users a provisional “processing” UI, then update status via polling or a follow‑up webhook.
Create a table with a UNIQUE idempotency_key column and index it to reject duplicate webhook events.
Use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (or MERGE) to atomically create the payment row only once.
Wrap the payment insert and balance update in a single transaction with SERIALIZABLE isolation to prevent races.
Add a status field (pending/processed) and receipt_timestamp to track webhook handling progress.
Return a short HTTP timeout (e.g., 2 s) to the client and enqueue the webhook for async processing.
If processing exceeds the timeout, respond 202 Accepted and let a background worker complete the transaction.
Assign a unique idempotency key (e.g., webhook event ID) and store it with the transaction status before processing.
On receipt, check the key; if already processed, return success without reapplying the charge.
Wrap balance update and idempotency record insertion in a single DB transaction for atomicity.
Send HTTP 200 only after the transaction commits, preventing premature retries from the sender.
Configure webhook endpoint timeout to a few seconds (e.g., 5 s) to keep UI responsive while work continues.
When events arrive out of order, process only if its timestamp is newer than the stored last‑processed time.
Assign each webhook a globally unique idempotency key (e.g., provider event ID) and store it with processing status.
Persist the idempotency record before any business logic to prevent race conditions on duplicate deliveries.
Make the payment handler idempotent: if a transaction with the same key exists, skip creation and return success.
Wrap database updates in a single ACID transaction with row‑level locking to guarantee only one charge per key.
Respond 200 OK to the webhook immediately after storing the event; perform the actual charge asynchronously.
Set a short HTTP timeout (e.g., 2 s) for webhook responses; longer work should be done in background workers.
Use a deduplication queue or message broker that serializes events per account to preserve order and avoid double‑charge.
Record event timestamps and sequence numbers; ignore or reorder out‑of‑order events based on these fields.
Validate webhook signatures and payload integrity before any processing to prevent forged duplicate requests.
